coredata.dc:title: Dual signal output ratiometric biosensor platform with regulable nanofiber-mediated signal amplification strategy for ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shibo
Zuo, Wanchao
Wu, Pengcheng
Meng, Xiangming
Sun, Caixia
Zhang, Donghui
Dai, Jianjun
Ju, Yanmin
coredata.dc:description: It is of great significance for the early diagnosis of cancer to detect the tumor biomarkers in human blood. However, it is still challenging to develop easily available and cost-effective methods to detect the ultralow-level tumor biomarkers. In this work, an innovative signal amplification strategy was developed for the ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers. Amphiphilic peptides with high self-assembly performance can self-assemble into regulable nanofibers and enrich fluorophore to amplify the signal, which can improve the sensitivity of detection. Meanwhile, the design of MOFs with higher fluorescence quenching efficiency and luminescence characteristics can realize double-signal output and further improve the accuracy and sensitivity of detection. Taking alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) as a model tumor biomarker, this strategy achieved a wide linear range of 1–40 ng/mL detection, reducing the limit of detection (LOD) from 9.02 ng/mL to 0.28 ng/mL compared with no signal amplification strategy. Significantly, the results of clinical samples detected by this strategy was consistent with chemiluminescence immunoassay used in clinical practice. Our work provides a brand-new idea for signal amplification strategy and shows an important application prospect in the early diagnosis of various diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic peptides
Amplification strategy
Nanofibers
Tumor biomarkers
Ultrasensitive detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of AFP in serum and clinical samples by the constructed FEPN-RFB
Synthesis and characterization of amphiphilic peptides
Amplification of fluorescence signal by amphiphilic peptides with different number of amino groups
Optimization of AFP detection based on FEPN-RFB
Detection of AFP in human serum samples
Detection of clinical samples by FEPN-RFB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of amphiphilic peptides. (A) Synthesis process of peptide nanofibers. (B) TEM image of peptide nanofibers. (C) 1H NMR of Ap-2. (D) FTIR spectroscopy of Ap-2. (E) XRD patterns of Ap-2. (F) CD spectra of Ap-2. (G) TGA (black line) and DTG (red line) of Ap-2.

Amplification of fluorescence signal performance of amphiphilic peptides with different number of amino groups. (A) FTIR spectroscopy of Apt-TAMRA, Ap-2, AA2. (B) Zeta potential of Apt-TAMRA, Ap-2, AA2. (C) Fluorescence spectra at Apt-TAMRA and AA1, AA2, AA3 containing 20 nM Apt-TAMRA, (D) 40 nM Apt-TAMRA, and (E) 60 nM Apt-TAMRA. (F) The signal amplification results of amphiphilic peptides with different number of amino groups under different Apt-TAMRA concentrations.

Synthesis and characterization of CQDs@MIL-101. (A) Synthesis process of CQDs@MIL-101. (B) SEM image of MIL-101. (C) TEM image of CQDs. (D) SEM image of CQDs@MIL-101. (E) XRD patterns of CQDs, MIL-101 and CQDs@MIL-101. (F) UV − vis absorption of CQDs@MIL-101, and the fluorescence spectra of Apt-TAMRA. (G) Fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra (λex = 530 nm) of CQDs@MIL-101 in aqueous solution.

Optimization of AFP detection. (A) Schematic representation of the AFP induced ratiometric fluorescence change (B) The optimization of concentration of CQDs@MIL-101. (C) The optimization of incubation time for AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101 (0.4 mg/mL). (D) The optimization of incubation time of the system after the addition of AFP (250 ng/mL). All experiments were performed in PBS buffer and the fluorescence spectra were all measured under excitation at 545 nm and 530 nm, respectively.

Ratiometric sensing of AFP by using FEPN-RFB and RFB. (A) The fluorescence spectra of the FEPN-RFB in the presence of different AFP concentrations from 0 to 40 ng/mL. (B) The linear relationship between the relative intensity and the concentration of AFP within the range from 1 ng/mL to 40 ng/mL based FEPN-RFB. (C) The linear relationship between the relative intensity and the concentration of AFP within the range from 10 ng/mL to 60 ng/mL based RFB. (D) The specificity of the FEPN-RFB. The concentration of BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA were all 100 ng/mL and the concentration of mixture of BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA, AFP was also 100 ng/mL. All experiments were performed in PBS buffer under excitation at 545 nm, emission at 580 nm and excitation at 530 nm, emission at 645 nm. (E) The reproducibility of the FPEN-RFB sensor was examined by detecting 10 ng/mL of AFP with five independent sensors at the same analysis condition. (F) The stability of AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101 fluorescence signals.

Detection of clinical samples. (A) Heat map showing the results of AFP detection in 25 clinical serum samples using FEPN-RFB method. Values represented in the heat map are the concentration of AFP. (B) Heat map showing the results of AFP detection in 25 clinical serum samples using CLIA method. (C) Correlation of detecting concentration of AFP for FEPN-RFB and CLIA. (D) FEPN-RFB evaluated 25 clinical serum samples for cancer. The “+” represents the positive sample confirmed by FEPN-RFB, the “−” represents the negative sample confirmed by FEPN-RFB. (E) CLIA evaluated 25 clinical serum samples for cancer. The “+” represents the positive sample confirmed by FEPN-RFB, the “−” represents the negative sample confirmed by FEPN-RFB. (F) Positive and negative percentage results in clinical serum samples by FEPN-RFB and CLIA.

Schematic illustration of the biosensor for fluorescence enhanced and detecting AFP. (A) Synthesis of peptide nanofibers and enhancement of fluorescence signal. (B) Schematic illustration of the process to detect AFP by FEPN-RFB compared with RFB.
Comparison of the analytical parameters for a variety of biosensors for AFP detection.
Determination of AFP in human serum by the biosensor.

head.ce:title: Dual signal output ratiometric biosensor platform with regulable nanofiber-mediated signal amplification strategy for ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Joint International Research Laboratory of Animal Health and Food Safety, Key Laboratory of Animal Bacteriology, Ministry of Agriculture, College of Veterinary Medicine, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095 PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Joint International Research Laboratory of Animal Health and Food Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Joint International Research Laboratory of Animal Health and Food Safety, Key Laboratory of Animal Bacteriology, Ministry of Agriculture, College of Veterinary Medicine, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing 210095 PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhanjiang Institute of Clinical Medicine, Central People's Hospital of Zhanjiang, Guangdong Medical University Zhanjiang Central Hospital, Zhanjiang 524045, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Medical University Zhanjiang Central Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhanjiang Institute of Clinical Medicine, Central People's Hospital of Zhanjiang, Guangdong Medical University Zhanjiang Central Hospital, Zhanjiang 524045, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great significance for the early diagnosis of cancer to detect the tumor biomarkers in human blood. However, it is still challenging to develop easily available and cost-effective methods to detect the ultralow-level tumor biomarkers. In this work, an innovative signal amplification strategy was developed for the ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers. Amphiphilic peptides with high self-assembly performance can self-assemble into regulable nanofibers and enrich fluorophore to amplify the signal, which can improve the sensitivity of detection. Meanwhile, the design of MOFs with higher fluorescence quenching efficiency and luminescence characteristics can realize double-signal output and further improve the accuracy and sensitivity of detection. Taking alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) as a model tumor biomarker, this strategy achieved a wide linear range of 1–40 ng/mL detection, reducing the limit of detection (LOD) from 9.02 ng/mL to 0.28 ng/mL compared with no signal amplification strategy. Significantly, the results of clinical samples detected by this strategy was consistent with chemiluminescence immunoassay used in clinical practice. Our work provides a brand-new idea for signal amplification strategy and shows an important application prospect in the early diagnosis of various diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a major public health problem worldwide, killing more than 9,960,000 people each year [1]. Early diagnosis of cancer can greatly improve the survival rate of patients [2]. At present, the detection of tumor biomarkers to diagnose and monitor tumor occurrence and progression has attracted wide attention. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is an important diagnostic tumor-specific biomarker for different types of cancer [3]. Therefore, the early detection of AFP levels in human blood would play a crucial role in the prevention of different cancer. The common method for AFP detection is fluorescence biosensor, which is a reliable, rapid and sensitive method for early detection [4]. However, due to the relatively low concentration of AFP in vivo, the sensitivity of traditional fluorescence biosensors cannot fully meet the requirements of AFP monitoring. Therefore, it is necessary to develop fluorescence biosensors with higher sensitivity to achieve trace analysis of tumor biomarkers.

Designing an accurate fluorescence biosensor with higher sensitivity should focus on two factors: signal amplification and dual signal output. The first factor is signal amplification. Common signal amplification strategies include rolling cycle amplification (RCA) [5], strand displacement reaction (SDR) [6], and enzyme-assisted amplification (EAA) [7], which have the disadvantages of specific amplification template and long-time reaction [8,9], design multiple probes [10], harsh reaction conditions, high temperature requirements, and high cost of enzymes [11]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a simple and easily available signal amplification strategy. Amphiphilic peptides are polypeptide molecules composed of hydrophobic amino acids and hydrophilic amino acids [12]. Due to the non-covalent bonding forces (such as aromatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions [13], etc.) between amphiphilic peptide molecules, they are considered as important materials for the construction of self-assembled nanostructures, and play an important role in cell imaging due to their flexibility, functionality, high biocompatibility and simple synthesis [14]. Wen et al., combined amphiphilic peptides with quantum dots and tumor targeting peptides, which not only specifically targeted cancer cells but also enhanced the fluorescence imaging effect of cells through self-assembly of amphiphilic peptides [15]; Jeena et al., integrated amphiphilic peptides with mitochondrial target molecules and fluorescence molecules, also achieving similar fluorescence imaging enhancement effect [16]. Although self-assembly amphiphilic peptides have shown unique advantages in vivo imaging, the application of amphiphilic peptides in vitro detection has not been reported. The combination of amphiphilic peptide with fluorescence signal is expected to increase the number of fluorophores per unit volume, further increasing the fluorescence signal through its self-assembly. As a result, amphiphilic peptides hold great potential for signal amplification of fluorescence biosensors.

The second factor is dual signal output. The fluorescence biosensor platform with dual signal output could realize accurate and ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers. In recent years, different types of nanomaterials have been used as platforms for fluorescence biosensors [17]. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) composed of metal ions and organic ligands have become a research hotspot in the field of fluorescence sensing [18]. In the process of fluorescence sensing, the fluorophore labeled aptamer can be adsorbed on the MOFs by van der Waals force, electrostatic or π-π interaction, resulting in fluorescence quenching of fluorophore [19]. In presence of the target, the aptamer is released from the MOFs due to the specific binding to the target, allowing fluorescence recovery of the fluorophore. However, during fluorescence intensity measurement, the effects of concentration, environment and excitation light intensity result in low accuracy of detection for the MOFs-based single signal fluorescence sensor. To ameliorate this limitation, reference signal is introduced to construct a MOFs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor [20]. Luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) with stable fluorescence signal output could be used to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensors. LMOFs can be generally prepared by using fluorescence ligands [21], introducing rare earth metal ions [22] or encapsulating nanoparticles [23]. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on LMOFs has great application potential for accurate and ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarkers.

Herein, we proposed a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor with signal amplification based on regulable amphiphilic peptide nanofibers for ultrasensitive detection of tumor biomarker (Scheme 1 ). The biosensor called FEPN-RFB was mainly constructed with the following steps: (1) Amphiphilic short peptide (Ap-2) with self-assembly property was synthesized by solid phase peptide synthesis (SPSS); (2) Ap-2 was covalently linked to the fluorophores labeled aptamer (Apt-TAMRA) to form AA2. (3) The LMOF (i.e. CQDs@MIL-101), was prepared by doping carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into MIL-101, endowing with fluorescence quenching capacity and luminescent properties. Notably, AA2 could form peptide nanofibers (PNF) through self-assembly, then fluorophores were further enriched and fluorescence signals were amplified. For AFP detection, first, AA2 was co-incubated with LMOF solution, and gradually adsorbed on the surface of LMOF. The fluorescence was quenched by LMOF due to the generation of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). After the addition of AFP, because of the higher affinity between the aptamer and the target, the aptamer was released from the LMOF surface, and the red fluorescence from the fluorophores was restored. Compared with the traditional fluorescence biosensor (RFB), FEPN-RFB reduced the detection limit from 9.02 ng/mL to 0.28 ng/mL for AFP, which was a 30-fold reduction. Compared with the traditional fluorescence signal amplification strategies, FEPN-RFB was easy to build and the entire detection cycle less than 2 h, enabling simple, fast, mild, convenient and efficient detection of biomarkers. Meanwhile, the results of clinical samples detected by FEPN-RFB were highly consistent with chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA) used in clinical practice. The amphiphilic peptide-based ratiometric fluorescence biosensing platform provides a brand-new idea for signal amplification strategy, and is promising for achieving trace detection of cancer markers and early diagnosis of various diseases.

Fmoc-protected amino acids were purchased from Nanjing Peptide Biotech Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), neutral red (NR), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBT), 1-Naphthylacetic acid (NAA), N, N-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), and 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) were gained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). 1,4-dicarboxybenzene (PTA), citric acid (CA), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and Wang resin were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Purified alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), and immunoglobulin G (Ig G) were provided by Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Human serum albumin (HSA) was obtained from Acmec Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Cr (NO3)3··9H2O was supplied by Nanjing Reagent (Nanjing, China). An ssDNA aptamer for AFP was employed as the targeting ligand and synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequence of the TAMRA-AFP aptamer (Apt) was 5′-TAMRA-GTGACGCTCCTAACGCTGACTCAGGTGCAGTTCTCGACTCGGTCTTGATGTGGGTCCTGTCCGTCCGAACCAATC-COOH-3′. All the reagents were of analytical grade and there was no further purification. The water used for all aqueous solutions was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm).

The compounds of amphiphilic peptide were synthesized according to a standard protocol of SPPS protocol. Briefly, Wang resins were swollen in dichloromethane (DCM,) and conjugated with the first Fmoc-amino acid, followed by capping unreacted sites with DCM and acetic anhydride (80:20 v/v). Then, elongation of peptide chains was performed by repeated steps of Fmoc deprotection with N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and piperidine (80:20 v/v) and conjugation of Fmoc-amino acid with the addition of DMAP/HOBT/DIC. Finally, TFA was used to cleave product from resins. The reaction solution was concentrated to get crude product, which was further purified by Shimadzu preparative High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) equipped with a reversed-phase C18 column.

For the synthesis of AA2, Ap-2 (5 mg/mL) and Apt (40 nM) were reacted with EDC (5 mg/mL) and NHS (5 mg/mL) at room temperature for 4 h. The obtained reaction solution was purified by dialysis. To explore the optimal magnification, amphiphilic peptides containing different numbers of amino groups were synthesized (Ap-1, Ap-2, Ap-3) according to the above method, and reacted with Apt to generate AA1, AA2, AA3, respectively.

Firstly, CQDs were synthesized according to previously reported methods [24], briefly, 1.5 g of citric acid and 2.1 mg of neutral red were dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water and stirred well. Then, the homogeneous solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After cooled to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove black solid precipitate, and then the supernatant was further filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The final solid-state CQDs were gained by freeze-drying.

Secondly, 3 mL CQDs (10 mg/mL), 1.6 g Cr (NO3)3·9H2O, 664 mg PTA and 0.18 mL hydrofluoric acid (HF) were dissolved in 19 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min to form a uniform mixture solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and heated at 220 ℃ for 8 h. The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The precipitates were washed alternately with warm DMF and ethanol for three times, later dried and stored in 4 ℃ for further use.

To compare the detection performance of the conventional RFB and the proposed FEPN-RFB in a typical procedure, 150 μL Apt (40 nM) or AA2 containing 40 nM Apt were separately mixed with 100 μL CQDs@MIL-101 (0.4 mg/mL) suspensions and incubated for 10 min. Subsequently, AFP was added to the system and kept incubating for another 90 min, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 580 nm before and after the addition of AFP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AFP in serum and clinical samples by the constructed FEPN-RFB
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the possible application of the proposed method, human serum samples were assayed using the standard addition method by the FEPN-RFB. The human serum samples from healthy adults were filtered through a 0.45 µm filter membrane and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatants were diluted 10 times using PBS before measurement. Three different concentrations of AFP were detected using the same assay described above, and recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated.

Clinical human serum samples were provided by the Zhanjiang Institute of Clinical Medicine, Central People's Hospital of Zhanjiang, including nine AFP-positive serum samples and sixteen AFP-negative serum samples. The human serum samples were centrifugated at 3500 rpm for 5 min, and then the supernatant was diluted with PBS. Then, the FEPN-RFB system was used to detect AFP in different serum samples under the same conditions according to the procedures described above. The proportion of positive sample was calculated by Zpositive = X/N*100%, where X was the number of positive samples, and N was the number of total samples. The proportion of negative sample was calculated by Znegative = Y/N*100%, where Y was the number of negative samples, and N was the number of total samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of amphiphilic peptides
ce:section.ce:para: First, amphiphilic peptides with self-assembly ability were synthesized by SPPS, and each amino acid sequence was covalently linked to the resin, respectively (Fig. 1 A). Then, the peptide sequence was cut from the resin by TFA to obtain the target peptide Ap-2 crude, which was further purified by HPLC. As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of Fig. 1B and Fig. S1, the Ap-2 could self-assemble to form cross-linked peptide nanofibers by π-π stacking in aqueous solution, and the width was about 32.6 nm.

The molecular weight of Ap-2 was 737.40118 in mass spectrum (Fig. S2), and the test results were consistent with the standard molecular weight. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 1C) showed that the characteristic peak at the chemical shift of 1.5 ppm was attributed to amino hydrogen, and the characteristic peak at the chemical shift of 7–8 ppm was attributed to benzene ring hydrogen. The 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S3) showed that the characteristic peaks at the chemical shift of 120–130 ppm and 170 ppm were assigned to the benzene ring carbon and carbonyl carbon, respectively. Both the 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were consistent with the peak position of the standard spectrum. In the Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR), the characteristic peaks at 3286 cm−1 and 3436 cm−1 corresponded to the N-H and the characteristic peaks at 1643 cm−1 was C = O. The characteristic peak at 1203 cm−1 was ascribed to C-N, and the peak at 726 cm−1 was benzene ring, which proved that the presence of amino, amide bond and benzene ring in the compound Ap-2 (Fig. 1D). From the UV absorption spectrum in Fig. S4, Ap-2 had an absorption peak at 280 nm, indicating a π → π * electronic transition between the benzene ring and amide bond of the compound. All the above results verified the successful synthesis of amphiphilic peptide Ap-2.

The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) (Fig. 1E) showed that Ap-2 had an obvious characteristic absorption peak at 20°, indicating that Ap-2 was an amorphous compound. Circular dichroism (CD) showed (Fig. 1F) that Ap-2 had a positive peak at 200–230 nm and a negative peak at 230–250 nm, indicating that the secondary structure of Ap-2 was β-Sheet conformation. The thermal stability of Ap-2 was evaluated, as shown in Fig. 1G. The thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) curve (black line) showed that the first thermal degradation of Ap-2 occurred with a weight loss of around 5.00%, which may be due to the evaporation of crystalline water. The second and last thermal degradation of Ap-2 occurred with a weight loss of around 21.30% and 56.17%, which were due to the breaking of peptide bonds. The derivative thermal gravimetric (DTG) curve (red line) showed that the temperatures of the maximum weight loss velocity of the three thermal degradation were 107℃, 233℃ and 360℃, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curve showed that the peaks were all downward, indicating the endothermic process occurred. Thus, the thermal degradation of Ap-2 was an endothermic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amplification of fluorescence signal by amphiphilic peptides with different number of amino groups
ce:section.ce:para: After the amphiphilic peptides were combined with the aptamer labeled fluorophore, its self-assembly ability could enrich the fluorophore to achieve fluorescence signal amplification [25]. However, the fluorescence intensity is not infinitely enhanced. Self-quenching occurred when fluorophore aggregated [26]. In order to verify the signal amplification capability of amphiphilic peptides, which were covalently bound to an aptamer labeled with fluorophore. FTIR (Fig. 2 A) showed that AA2 had characteristic peaks of Apt-TAMRA, and the amino peak of Ap-2 disappeared, which proved the successful synthesis of AA2. The Zeta potential (Fig. 2B) showed that Apt-TAMRA was − 11.77 mV, Ap-2 was + 25.77 mV, and AA2 was + 22.83 mV. The zeta potential indicated the successful combination of Apt-TAMRA and Ap-2. To verify the feasibility of signal amplification, firstly the electrophoretic experiment was studied. As shown in Fig. S6, Apt-TAMRA had a bright band, while Ap-2 and AA2 did not produce this phenomenon, indicating that Apt-TAMRA successfully combined with Ap-2 to form nanofibers. Next, the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S7) showed that the fluorescence intensity of AA2 was stronger than Apt-TAMRA, indicating that amphiphilic peptides could achieve fluorescence signal amplification.

In order to obtain the optimal conditions for fluorescence signal amplification of peptide nanofibers, the effects of the number of amino groups in amphiphilic peptide and the concentration of Apt-TAMRA modification were investigated. Another two amphiphilic peptides (Ap-1, Ap-3) with different number of amino groups were synthesized by SPPS. The mass spectrometry results showed (Fig. S8) that the molecular weights of Ap-1 and Ap-3 were consistent with the standard molecular weights, and the peak position of 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S9) was also consistent with the standard spectrum. FTIR (Fig. S10) showed that the characteristic peaks of Ap-1 and Ap-3 were consistent with the standard peaks. Therefore, Ap-1 and Ap-3 were synthesized successfully. Then, Ap-1, Ap-2 and Ap-3 were reacted with the Apt-TAMRA to form AA1, AA2 and AA3 with different concentrations of Apt-TAMRA (20 nM, 40 nM and 60 nM), respectively. Compared with the fluorescence of 20 nM Apt-TAMRA alone, as the number of amino groups in amphiphilic peptide raised, AA2 showed the most significant enhancement of the fluorescence signal, while AA3 was weaker than that of AA2 because fluorescence self-quenching occurred (Fig. 2C). The results were consistent when Apt-TAMRA concentration was 40 nM (Fig. 2D). When the Apt-TAMRA concentration was 60 nM, the fluorescence signal of AA1 decreased, which fluorescence self-quenching also occurred (Fig. 2E). The slight change of the peak pattern of TAMRA was due to the change of the charge distribution the fluorophore molecules [27] and the fluorophore molecules arrangement [28]. The amplification effects of fluorescence signals under different conditions were clearly summarized in Fig. 2G, with an increasing concentration of Apt-TAMRA, the fluorescence magnification increased and then decreased. With the increase of the number of amino groups in amphiphilic peptides, the fluorescence magnification increased and then decreased. The maximum fluorescence magnification up to 160% could be achieved when Ap-2 was covalently bound with 40 nM Apt-TAMRA forming AA2. The fluorescence rate of change was calculated by (F’-F)/F, where F was the fluorescence intensity of Apt-TAMRA, and F’ was the fluorescence intensity of AA1/AA2/AA3 at the same Apt-TAMRA concentration. Thus, AA2 was further used to carry out subsequent detection tests (Fig. S11).

CQDs@MIL-101 nanocomposites were prepared by two-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 3 A). First, CQDs were synthesized by heating CA and NR at 180 °C for 8 h (Fig. S12), and then heated CQDs, terephthalic acid and Cr (NO3)3·9H2O were heated at 220 °C for 8 h to obtain CQDs@MIL-101 nanocomposites. TEM image (Fig. 3C) showed that the CQDs were spherical with a diameter of 3 nm. From the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of MIL-101 and CQDs@MIL-101 in Fig. 3B and 3D, MIL-101 was a regular octahedral structure, and the surface was smooth and non-grainy, and CQDs@MIL-101 retained its octahedral structure, but the surface was rough and grainy, indicating that CQDs were successfully doped in MIL-101 (Fig. S13). Also, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) results showed that the particle size increased from 723 nm to 789 nm with the doping of CQDs (Fig. S14).

Zeta potential results showed that MIL-101, CQDs, and CQDs@MIL-101 were all positively charged (Fig. S15). XRD patterns showed that the absorption peaks of CQDs and MIL-101 were retained in CQDs@MIL-101 nanocomposites, further indicating the successful synthesis of CQDs@MIL-101 (Fig. 3E). The Fig. 3F showed that CQDs@MIL-101 had a wide absorption at 560–640 nm, and Apt had maximum excitation at 580 nm, indicating that the adsorption peak of CQDs@MIL-101 overlapped well with the fluorescence emission of Apt. To better verify the effect of MIL-101 on Apt-TAMRA fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence spectrum of MIL-101 quenching Apt-TAMRA fluorescence was studied. As shown in Fig. S16, the Apt-TAMRA solution alone had a very strong fluorescence signal. When the same concentration of Apt-TAMRA was incubated with CQDs@MIL-101, the fluorescence of Apt-TAMRA decreased because FRET between Apt-TAMRA and CQDs@MIL-101 occurred. Thus, CQDs@MIL-101 could act as an effective fluorescence quenching agent for Apt-TAMRA, resulting in FRET between Apt-TAMRA and CQDs@MIL-101. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3G) showed that CQDs@MIL-101 had maximum excitation at 530 nm and maximum emission at 645 nm, thus CQDs@MIL-101 could be used as a luminescent nanomaterial to provide a reference signal for the detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of AFP detection based on FEPN-RFB
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from the Fig. 4 A, CQDs@MIL-101 had an emission peak at 645 nm, which could quench the fluorescence intensity of AA2 through FRET process due to the adsorption of AA2 on its surface [29]. With the addition of the AFP, FRET was blocked because AA2 was far from the surface of CQDs@MIL-101, thus the fluorescence of AA2 was restored. It is worth noting that the fluorescence of the CQDs@MIL-101 at 645 nm were basically unchanged, indicating that the value of the system reference signal did not change with the change of AFP. Therefore, the FEPN-RFB system could sensitively detect AFP.

Some factors can affect the detection performance of FEPN-RFB system. To establish a highly sensitive and accurate detection system, it is important to optimize several essential parameters including the concentration of CQDs@MIL-101, the incubation time of AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101, and the incubation time of the system after the addition of AFP. Relative fluorescence intensity can be used as a measure of parameter optimization and is calculated by (F1-F0)/F0, where F1 is the ratiometric fluorescence intensity in the presence of AFP, and F0 is the ratiometric fluorescence intensity without AFP. As shown in Fig. 4B, with increasing of the concentration of CQDs@MIL-101 from 0.1 to 0.4 mg/mL, the relative fluorescence intensity increased gradually. This phenomenon was due to the fact that high concentration of CQDs@MIL-101 increased the adsorption capacity for AA2. As the concentration of CQDs@MIL-101 continued to increase, the relative fluorescence intensity decreases, which was due to the increasing difficulty in binding AA2 with AFP. So, 0.4 mg/mL of CQDs@MIL-101 was chosen as the best dosage for the following experiments. The incubation time of AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101 would also affect the analytical performances for the detection of AFP. As shown in Fig. 4C, with increasing of the incubation time, the relative fluorescence intensity increased first and then decreased, and reached the maximum at 10 min. Thus, 10 min was chosen as the optimized incubation time for CQDs@MIL-101 and AA2. The last factor was the incubation time of the system after the addition of AFP. As shown in Fig. 4D, with a longer time, more AA2 on the surface of the CQDs@MIL-101 were supposed to be bound with AFP, and the relative fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at 90 min. Therefore, 90 min was chosen as the optimized incubation time.

Under optimized experimental conditions, different concentrations of AFP were added to the FEPN-RFB system to calculate sensitivity. The results of Fig. 5 A showed the fluorescence emission spectrum of intensity of Apt-TAMRA and CQDs@MIL-101 at different AFP concentrations of 0–40 ng/mL. With the increase of AFP concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Apt-TAMRA gradually increased. This due to the addition of AFP enhanced the affinity between AFP and AA2, leading to the separation of AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101, and the fluorescence intensity of Apt-TAMRA was restored. It was worth that the fluorescence intensity of CQD@MIL-101 kept relatively stable, indicating that the reference signal was not affected by AFP. Observe the relationship between fluorescence intensity and concentration by calculating the relative fluorescence intensity. The Fig. 5B results showed that in the range of 1–40 ng/mL, the relative fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with AFP concentration and maintained a good linear relationship. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.28 ng/mL. The LOD was calculated by CLOD = 3σ/K, where σ was the standard deviation of blank measurements (n = 3), and K was the slope of the linear regression equation. Such a low detection limit was due to amplification of the fluorescence signal by amphiphilic peptides, which increased the sensitivity of the fluorescence signal from quenching to recovery.

To evaluate the extent to which FEPN-RFB reduced the detection limit, tested the detection limit of the RFB system under the same conditions. The only change was that the aptamer used by the RFB system did not bind to amphiphilic peptides. The Fig. 5C result showed that the RFB detection system had a good linear relationship in the range of 10–60 ng/mL, and the LOD was 9.02 ng/mL. Compared with RFB, FEPN-RFB had more than 30 times lower detection limits, thanks to amphiphilic peptides. The FEPN-RFB detection system was compared with previously reported aptamer sensors for AFP detection. As shown in Table 1 , the electrochemical aptasensors have low detection limits, however, the entire experiment requires complex electrode modifications and has poor selectivity. While FRET-based aptamer nanoprobes offer advantages in detection speed and applicability. Compared with other aptamer fluorescence biosensors, FEPN-RFB has a lower detection limit than the vast majority, which is the role of amphiphilic peptides. Therefore, fluorescence signal amplification plays a crucial role in fluorescence biosensors, and a good signal amplification strategy of amphiphilic peptides will provide a new way of thinking.

To verify the specificity of FEPN-RFB, the proteins including BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA and the protein mixture of BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA, AFP were added into serum and applied in the detection system. As shown in Fig. 5D, the fluorescence increased significantly when AFP was added at a concentration of 100 ng/mL, while the fluorescence did not increase for the same concentration of 100 ng/mL BSA, HSA, IgG, and CEA. In addition, the fluorescence of protein mixture increased equally with the same concentration of AFP. These results showed that FEPN-RFB had good specificity for AFP, in which the high specific affinity between AA2 and AFP played an important role. To verify the reproducibility of FEPN-RFB, AFP at a concentration of 10 ng/mL was detected with five independent detection systems under the same conditions, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) obtained was 2.52% (Fig. 5E), indicating that the reproducibility of the proposed biosensor was acceptable. The sensor system was stored at room temperature and its stability was performed. After 7 days, 14 days, 21 days, the fluorescence signals of AA2 and CQDs@MIL-101 remained largely unchanged (Fig. 5F). The results showed that the prepared biosensor had good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AFP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of sensor for real sample testing, the FEPN-RFB was used for determining the recoveries of different concentrations of AFP by standard additive methods in human serum samples. First, the standard curve of AFP in serum was determined. As shown in Fig. S17A, in the range of 1–40 ng/mL, the concentration of AFP in serum still maintained a good linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity. Next, five different concentrations of AFP were added into the serum and detected. The results were shown in Table 2 , the RSD remained below 8% and the recoveries ranged from 95.00% to 102.76%, which were within the acceptable range. At the same time, we made the AFP concentrations of adding and determination for correlation analysis (Fig. S17B), and the results showed a good correlation (R2 = 0.99567), indicating that the FEPN-RFB detection platform had good accuracy. To further verify the specificity of FEPN-RFB in human serum, the proteins including BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA and protein mixture of BSA, HSA, IgG, CEA, AFP in human serum were added into serum and applied in the detection system (Fig. S18). The results showed that FEPN-RFB had good specificity for AFP in human serum. These results showed that the designed FEPN-RFB had broad application prospects in practical sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of clinical samples by FEPN-RFB
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of FEPN-RFB in complex biological samples, we performed an analysis of clinical samples. In addition, to assess the confidence of this assay, comparisons were made with magnetic particle chemiluminescence (CLIA) used clinically. We used FEPN-RFB to detect 25 serum samples clinically, and used 25 ng/mL as the cut-off concentration to judge whether the sample was negative or positive. As shown in Fig. 6 A, AFP concentrations in 25 clinical serum samples were detected by the FEPN-RFB method. The heat map showed that 9 samples had AFP concentrations above 25 ng/mL, thus the FEPN-RFB test result was 9 positive and 16 negative out of 25 clinical serum samples (Fig. 6D). The CLIA test results used clinically showed that there were also 9 samples with AFP concentrations of more than 25 ng/mL, thus the CLIA test results were 9 positive samples and 16 negative samples (Fig. 6B and Fig. 6E). At the same time, the two methods detected the same positive sample, indicating that the detection performance of the two methods was consistent. We selected the AFP concentrations detected by the two methods for correlation analysis (Fig. 6C), and the results showed that the two methods had a good correlation (R2 = 0.99728), and the proportion of positive and negative samples detected by the two methods were 36% and 64%, which further indicated that the results of the two methods were highly consistent (Fig. 6F). The above results indicate that FEPN-RFB has good clinical application value.

In summary, a simple and efficient dual signal output ratiometric fluorescence biosensor with signal amplification based on regulable nanofibers was developed to detect AFP. We introduced a novel signal amplification strategy for the first time by covalently binding amphiphilic peptides with fluorophore labeled aptamers, which enhanced the fluorescence signal through enrichment effects, greatly improving the sensitivity of the sensor. This amphiphilic peptide-based signal amplification process is spontaneous and does not need external force to drive. Especially, the amphiphilic peptide can be flexibly regulable and used for sensitivity improvement of different fluorophores or fluorescent nanomaterials or probes. Compared with other signal amplification strategies, this strategy has the advantages of easy synthesis, low cost, mild reaction conditions. The solid phase peptide synthesis method of amphiphilic peptide has been widely used and the amount synthesized at one time can meet the needs of the whole detection process. Furthermore, the whole process can be done in two hours at room temperature and does not require the use of high cost of enzymes.

Meanwhile, the construction of dual signal output ratiometric fluorescence biosensor can eliminate the influence of concentration, environment and excitation light intensity during detection and enhance the accuracy of detection results. The sensor had good detection performance, with a wide linear range of 1–40 ng/mL, and a low detection limit of 0.28 ng/mL, which was 30 times lower than RFB. In addition, the strategy had good specificity, acceptable reproducibility and stability. In the detection of clinical samples, the results of this method were consistent with the CLIA used in clinical practice. The satisfactory results of this work indicate that the proposed method is promising for AFP detection and it will have potential application in early screening or clinical diagnosis of cancer. Meaningfully, the innovative signal amplification strategy proposed in this study also plays an important foundation for constructing signal self-enhanced biosensors for other biomarkers detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20200574), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52002402, No. 32172855).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144026.

ce:section.ce:para:
